data.title: AIPI: A Well-Constructed Fund With Some Elevated Risk
data.description:
The best investments should be able to stand the test of time. While many companies and funds perform well in favorable market conditions, adversity often shows weaknesses more clearly. The market has sold off in an orderly way, but equities have still fallen notably in a short period of time since President Trump unveiled his long-term strategy of using tariffs to renegotiate trade agreements and bring manufacturing back to the United States.

A particular investment that has been impacted significantly by the recent increase in overall market volatility levels has been covered-call funds. These have become more commonplace and evolved significantly over the last decade, and the payouts and total returns these investments offer vary more than most equities during sell-offs.

One well-constructed new covered-call fund that came to market recently in June 2024 is the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:AIPI).

AIPI has offered investors total returns of negative 8.19 percent since the fund's inception in June 2024. The S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of negative 3.16 percent during this same time period, and the Nasdaq 100 has offered investors total returns of negative 6.41 percent since June 2024.

Before the recent sell-off, AIPI also performed essentially in line with the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100, while markets were rising as well.

Today, I am initiating coverage of the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF with a buy rating. This technology-based fund has performed well since the fund's inception in June 2024 in both more favorable market conditions and during the recent sell-off. AIPI uses an effective options strategy that sells out-of-the-money calls, and the fund focuses on large-cap tech companies that should continue to outperform the broader indexes for multiple reasons.

AIPI has an expense ratio of .65 percent, $238 million in assets under management, and a trailing yield of 36.31 percent. The fund seeks to track the BITA AI Leaders Select Index, which is composed of companies that have a high relative amount of revenue from artificial intelligence activities and services. AIPI's top holdings are NVIDIA (NVDA), Palantir Technologies Inc. Ordinary Shares - Class A (PLTR), ARM Holdings PLC ADR (ARM), and CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. Class A (CRWD). These four positions make up nearly 36 percent of the fund's overall holdings. AIPI is a large-cap tech fund.

The strategy that AIPI uses is to sell slightly out-of-the-money monthly call options against all of the fund's equity positions every month. The ETF adjusts the strike price of the options contracts sold based on market conditions.

The strategy of selling out-of-the-money calls has been a more effective strategy used by covered-call funds to minimize risks to the net asset value of these types of funds. Exchange-traded funds such as the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Core Premium Income ETF (GPIX) that sell out-of-the-money calls have offered investors better overall returns by minimizing losses to the principal with this strategy that enables investors to capture more of the upside of the underlying equity holdings.

AIPI is a large-cap tech fund that has performed largely in-line with the Nasdaq 100 during market rallies, and the ETF has only slightly performed tech-based indexes such as the Nasdaq 100 during sell-offs. Covered-call funds are not designed to outperform equities long-term, most income investors are willing to sacrifice on overall returns to get consistent and substantive income.

AIPI has paid out both significant and consistent income since the fund's inception in June 2024. The ETF's payouts have ranged from $1.28 a share to $1.51 a share. This investment's monthly payouts should remain high in the current more volatile market as while since the options premiums are up.

All investments have risks, and AIPI is a new fund with limited data that is not well-diversified. The ETF's top 4 holdings are nearly 36 percent of the fund, and large-cap tech has more international exposure to the current trade conflict right now in addition to this sector's significant operations in China. Still, big-cap tech has outperformed most of the major indexes over the last decade, and corporate balance sheets remain strong, these companies will likely keep spending to increase efficiency.

With increasing signs that the economy is weakening and Trump taking a hardline on trade, markets should remain volatile in the near term. While excessive levels of volatility can hurt principal, Trump is using tariffs to renegotiate longer-term trade agreements and I believe big cap tech earnings have held up relatively well even during periods of weakness. While AIPI won't likely outperform the broader indexes, this fund should continue to offer consistent income and solid total returns.

data.title: WesBanco: Acquisition Completed And Slightly Undervalued
data.description:
Thesis

I rate WesBanco, Inc. (NASDAQ:WSBC) as a "Buy," mainly because of the recent "Tariff Selloff" that made the stock trade at 8.4x Price to Earnings Fwd (Non-Gaap). I see potential upside of 20% for the next 12 months. I also think there may be some merger synergies due to cost savings, but I'm a little bit more skeptical about that. Also, it's performing slightly better than its peers. Today, April 7th, is ranked 48th out of 685 in Financials on Seeking Alpha and 13th out of 250 in Regional Banks. That implies that WesBanco has a higher score than 93% of financials and more than 95% of regional banks. Quant score right now is 4.74, rating it as a "Strong Buy".

As we can see in the quant summary, it's screaming a "BUY" or even a "STRONG BUY" but there are two issues: one is profitability. Return on Equity is 5.62% when the same industry median is at 10.44%.

The second issue is the book value. In some websites, for example, in the Seeking Alpha's valuation section, price-to-book is around 0.7x, but on YCharts and others, the same ratio is around 1x.

The reason for this difference is that the former book value does not take into account the issuing of shares to buy Premier Financial. For each stock owned of Premier Financial, they received 0.8 of WesBanco. Total outstanding shares of WesBanco, prior to the acquisition, were 66,919,805. Now they are 95,670,246, implying an increase of 42%.

Business Overview and Latest Financial Results

WesBanco, Inc. is a diversified financial holding, and "WesBanco Bank" is its primary subsidiary. Founded in 1870, it offers different kinds of financial services, mainly banking, but also wealth management and insurance services. If we follow the general approach to banks, we may separate their two segments in revenues: Net interest income and Non-interest income. The first one represents 80% on average for the last 10 years.

When we look at revenues in more detail, despite the loan growth of 4.15% CAGR, revenues have been flat in the last 5 years.

However, on August 1, 2023, Jeffrey H. Jackson became the new CEO and president of the company. He had previously joined WesBanco as Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer on August 15, 2022. Eleven months after that, the company proposes a merger. Maybe the winds of change are coming, and we will see some new growth in revenues ahead.

From a different perspective, the company, in its 10-K, classifies its segments into two: "community banking" and "trust and investment services".

The community banking segment accounts for 98% of total revenues and 105.6% of profit margin. In this case, is above the total profit margin because the "corporate other" segment contains expenses that belong to the parent company, and since this segment doesn't generate income on its own, the net result is negative. The Trust & Investment segment represents 4.4% of total revenues and 1.3% of total profits (both percentages, averaging the last 3 years).

A red flag that we can note is that the profit margin is falling, from 32% in 2022 to 26% in 2024.

However, not all is gloomy here. On February 28th, the company completed the acquisition of Premier Financial. It was a tender offer since it was approved by 85% of WesBanco's stockholders and 68% of Premier Financial's stockholders. I will delve into it in the merger analysis section.

Total loans grew 8.8% YoY. The core section, like most banks, is the commercial real estate, which includes "Land and construction" and "Improved property," representing 57.8% of total Loans and growing at 11.5% YoY. Land and Construction is the star sector, growing 28%, and 'consumer loans' is the worst, falling by 12.3%.

When we perform an age analysis of loans, we can notice two things. The first is that in 2023, total past due represented 0.43% of total loans, and at the end of 2024, that percentage rose to 0.7%, which implies an increase of 78% YoY of total past due loans, or 27 basis points.

Second, the two segments that have increased their past due portion are "Improved property" and "Commercial and Industrial" ones, and for the former, its past-due loans have been increasing in the last 90 days, for the 2024 fiscal year.

At first glance, the numbers look fine if we take into account the national delinquency mean of 3.6% for Q4 2024. Here, delinquency is defined as 90 days or more.

The company is an outstanding place to work, according to the polls of Newsweek. Nearly 95 percent said their work is "fulfilling", and approximately 90 percent said they had recommended WesBanco as a "great place to work" to people outside the organization.

Also, in different metrics, the company has shown to be better than its peers in its asset quality.

When it comes to interest rates, the market has changed a lot in the last days. A week ago, the market was pricing a probability of only 15.6% for the FED to cut down the rates to the 4.00-4.25 range in the next meeting on May 7th; now the probability is 46.1%. Also, if you look further in time, for example, for December 17th, 2025, the implicit interest rate in the market price is around 3.5%.

Interest rates are critical in banks that are "asset-sensitive". When the interest rates rise, banks can reprice the new loans, and if they don't reprice at the same speed as the deposits, the Net Interest Margin [NIM] can improve, and that's the reason that, in most cases, rising rates are a pretty good thing for banks. The opposite may be true in some cases, falling rates put some pressure on the NIM, and banks struggle more to be profitable. In short, the macroeconomic context doesn't shed any light on WesBanco as other banks and companies.

In the last earnings call, the CFO claimed that the assets of WesBanco are pretty much neutral to changes in interest rates. I think that could be true to a great extent, but we should remain aware of the macroeconomic context. Also, they are modelling in their guidance and pro-forma earnings two more cuts of the FED. So, if that's the case, two more cuts may not be a big deal, but I insist, we should be concerned about that.

Having said that, they are anticipating 4 to 6 basis points of improvement on the NIM, for Q1 2025 and a little bit more for Q2 2025, due that 1 billion of Certificates of Deposits are maturing, and they expect to reprice them at a higher rate.

Merger Analysis

According to Prof. Damodaran, "Acquisitions are great for target companies, but not always for acquiring company stockholders".

That is precisely what happened with Premier Financial Corp, the target company in this case. The stock price soared between 30% and 40% in the days before the announcement of the acquisition on July 26th.

According to Prof. Damodaran, there are only 3 cases when the acquisition/merger creates value.

The first is operating synergies, like economies of scale (eg, both companies can dispense with several installations and use the same one for both companies, saving costs). Another one is the economics of scope, maybe some services exist in one company but not in the other, and they can begin offering these services to both, and that may deliver some additional growth.

A second way is financial synergies, combined companies may have a higher capacity to borrow debt, and they may use it to expand the business. Also, in some cases, they have tax benefits.

The third way is when the target company is potentially undervalued. The acquisition can be a driver that may lead to a more reasonable valuation, meanwhile, shareholders of the bidder company benefit from the rise in prices.

In this case, the argument put forth by management is that they expect to have operating synergies, more specifically, saving costs. Both management and analysts (consensus) are expecting around a 40% rise in EPS for 2025 and a 17% rise for 2026.

Here we can see it in more detail.

Delving deep into Pro-Forma EPS for 2025, management expects a 42% accretion, which implies a P/E of 7.73 for 2025. To get this number, they are excluding one-time charges (27.1 million) and fully phased in the Run-Rate saving costs (31 million). However, despite being a "one-time charge", the company will have to pay for them inevitably, and we don't know for sure how much time it would take to save these 31.5 million in costs. Alternatively, a more realistic case is the 27% accretion that they are expecting, which gives us a P/E of 8.57 for 2025.

I'm somewhat concerned about the "Fair Market Value" adjustments. They represent 68% of the EPS accretion expected and 22% of the pro forma earnings. However, this may not be something to worry about, but we should be aware of it and the magnitude of it. Banks have to do very often these kinds of adjustments, to reflect the current conditions of the market, especially interest rates.

Wrapping up, my opinion on the merger is slightly positive, not as strong as management and analysts expect. Combining the two companies can help save some costs, it is highly reasonable. However, on the other hand, the acquisition, as well as any other, carries some risks, for example, that the two companies' teams don't get along very well with each other. But, as of now, it's a potential problem, and if it occurs, it can be handled. Having stated that, I think that the "small" benefits of the acquisition outweigh the risks of it.

Valuation

Looking at the price to book ratio, it's average, in both ways compared with its peers, but also on a historical basis. Regarding price to book, as I discussed in the thesis section, we are going to see in some places something around 0.7x, but the right levels are around 1x.

The different peer companies that I used to compare to WesBanco are: Peoples Bancorp (PEBO), TowneBank (TOWN), First Commonwealth Financial Corporation (FCF), Trustmark Corporation (TRMK), and Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB). They are regional banks and have around the same market cap.

And the other bad issue about this bank is, Return on Equity is substantially below its peers.

The only ratio that can seem appealing is P/E forward, in both ways, when compared to peers and compared to history.

Compared to its peers is something interesting, but when compared to the historical average, it shows a strong potential undervaluation. But keep in mind that the EPS used is assuming an optimistic view of the acquisition, where the cost savings are successful, and also the fair market adjustments are "realistic".

As a rule of thumb, if P/E forward reverses to the 10-year historical mean of 12.8x from its current level of 8.4x, we can expect a 52% upside. But if we take the 3-year historical mean of 11.4x, the upside is 35%.

Obviously, these targets are very "spicy", and they are based on the premise of a successful EPS accretion, which I'm not fully convinced.

Risks

There are several topics that we can list as risks, for example, commercial real estate exposure, loan quality deterioration, mark-to-market losses, changes in regulations, etc, but the 3 main risks that I consider the most critical right now are: probability of recession, flattening or inverting of the yield curve and deposit outflows.

Goldman Sachs just hiked the odds of recession for the next year to 45%, J. P. Morgan even a starker 60% probability, and the New York FED Model is showing 30% at the end of March.

Concerning the yield curve, maybe right now it is not so flat or even inverted as, for example, one year ago. But the near short-term portion that includes between a couple of months and up to 2 years is already inverted. This curve is critical to banks, due that they usually borrow at a short term and lower rate, and then they may lend money at a longer term and higher rate. If the curve gets inverted, it puts a lot of pressure on the Net Income Margin and compromises profitability.

Regarding the risk of deposit outflows, we can take, for example, the bank crisis of March-May 2023. Several banks have suffered from several problems, but one critical issue was the withdrawal of deposits. In the following picture, we can note the drop in total deposits. If we take June 2022 to June 2023, the drop was 3.8% for all commercial banks in the US. But when it comes to individual banks, some of them didn't make it, and others were very complicated.

In the case of WesBanco, total deposits fell 5.21% in the same period. Interest-bearing deposits rose 1.7%, non-interest-bearing fell 9.5%, and institutional deposits dropped an astonishing 75%. This didn't move the needle because institutional deposits only represented 1-3% of total deposits, but that was not a good sign from the institutional.

Having said that, after analyzing the company, I don't find big problems or risks "out of the norm" inside the company, but I note the macroeconomic and systemic risks and their possible impact on WesBanco.

Conclusion

Wrapping up, in my opinion, it's a solid Bank, but it's not growing as fast and doesn't have the profitability that I would like. However, there are some reasons, like the acquisition and the new CEO, that make me give it a chance to the company, and maybe we can expect better figures regards to growth and profitability. As I said before, I'm a little bit skeptical about the synergies, and I'm also concerned about the macroeconomic context.

I'm going to value this company in two different ways, first using P/E Non-GAAP Fwd and Price to Book.

P/E Non Gaap TTM is 10.34 and P/E Non Gaap Fwd is 8.46, which implies an upside of 22% for the next 12 months.

Second, the "correct" price to book value "after acquisition" is 0.99 as of today, April 9th (I already discussed the difference vs the standalone ratio at the 0.7x level in the "thesis section"). The median of the sector is 1.09, which implies an upside of 10%. And if we add the ROE of 5-7%, we can deduce a target of 15-17% for the next 12 months.

So, averaging these two targets, I conclude my target at $33 per share, a 20% upside for the next 12 months.

data.title: Our Portfolio Managers Weigh Impact Of Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
Markets were already on edge, and sweeping new tariffs from President Trump have added another level of complexity. To help investors make sense of the implications, we asked our portfolio managers two key questions:

  1. How do the new tariffs impact your asset class?
  2. What indicators should investors be watching?

Their answers offer timely perspective on this shifting environment and provide valuable context to help investors cut through the noise.

Digital Assets: De-dollarization Moves Bitcoin Towards Monetary Role

Matthew Sigel

Impact

The Trump administration’s April 2 tariff package—targeting imports from China and the EU—has reignited global trade tensions and increased the risk of monetary and geopolitical fragmentation. Bitcoin initially dipped from $85k to the $81K range after the announcement and sold off further over the weekend, but has still outperformed the Nasdaq across every major timeframe—1 week, 1 month, YTD, and over the past 1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 years. While slower growth alone isn’t bullish for Bitcoin, the policy response might be: if tariffs weigh on GDP without triggering a fresh inflation spike, the Fed may have scope to cut rates—reintroducing the liquidity conditions under which Bitcoin has historically excelled. At the same time, the weaponization of trade and financial infrastructure continues to drive interest in neutral settlement rails.

That interest is no longer theoretical. China and Russia have reportedly begun settling some energy transactions in Bitcoin and other digital assets. Bolivia has announced plans to import electricity using crypto. And French energy utility EDF is exploring whether it can mine Bitcoin with surplus electricity currently exported to Germany. These are early signs that Bitcoin is evolving from a speculative asset into a functional monetary tool—particularly in economies looking to bypass the dollar and reduce exposure to U.S.-led financial systems.

Indicators to Watch

Investors should watch the evolving path of Fed policy: dovish shifts in rate expectations and rising liquidity are historically positive for Bitcoin. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) remains a key signal—any sustained dollar weakness may bolster the Bitcoin-as-hedge narrative, particularly in an environment of geopolitical fragmentation. Importantly, while 10-year Treasury yields surged on Monday, Bitcoin’s reaction was notably subdued. Unlike in 2022, rising yields did not trigger a wave of forced liquidations or volatility in crypto markets, suggesting that BTC may be decoupling from old macro sensitivities. Bitcoin ETP flows and on-chain activity also matter: despite recent volatility, U.S.-listed spot Bitcoin ETPs are still net positive by ~$600 million year-to-date, with renewed inflows in late March. Finally, watch for retaliatory moves from China or the EU—especially those aimed at bypassing dollar-based systems—which could accelerate adoption of crypto as an alternative settlement layer.

Gold: Safe Haven Demand Rises and Miners’ Margins Are Key

Imaru Casanova

Impact

Gold and gold stocks should ultimately benefit from the heightened level of risk across the global economy and global financial system. The unpredictability of economic policies and heightened market volatility should boost gold's appeal as the preferred safe-haven asset during times of global uncertainty. This should support a shift in investor sentiment towards gold and related equities.

Gold stocks’ leverage to the gold price, combined with their attractive valuations relative to the broader equity markets, and their low correlation with most other asset classes, should lead to a re-rating of the sector as investors look for a safer place to rotate capital to and as they look to diversify their portfolios.

Indicators to Watch

For the gold miners, investors should be focused on margins, assessing the impact of the ongoing trade war on global currencies and inflation trends for the sector. A weaker local currency leads to lower USD costs and improves margins for the miners. Gold miners were estimating cost inflation would average about 3-5% in 2025, prior to the recent tariff related developments.

Natural Resources: Sector Readies Response Playbook

Shawn Reynolds

Impact

Needless to say, the current tariff landscape is highly variable, complex and far from certain. Threats of further steps against China and their response ratchet up the intensity. Nevertheless, there is more clarity today than during February and March, even if it is still a blurry sight and worse than feared. However, given the severe reaction of global markets to tariff risks, we feel a large portion of the downside has been priced.

Undoubtedly, the biggest factor facing the entire natural resource sector is the current sentiment that a trade war will lead to a prolonged and deep global recession. In particular, the U.S. relationship with China has escalated and an off-ramp may take time and appear difficult to construct. Under that scenario, commodity demand of all of the main sectors covered by our Global Resources Strategy—energy (traditional and transitional), metals and mining and agriculture/paper/forest products—would suffer.

However, we believe the reality of application of “reciprocal” tariffs, as well as “retaliatory” tariffs will not be as disruptive as the worst fears. The broad-based 10% tariff influences the price of most commodities well within their recent ranges and is something to which the global economy historically has had a rather benign reaction relative to current perceptions. Additionally, geopolitical events over the last several years have reoriented and made supple commodity supply chains. The energy, metals and agricultural industries have adapted adroitly, and we believe these ecosystems will continuously evolve to newly optimal patterns. A good example of this is the 34% tit-for-tat tariffs on energy (mostly liquefied natural gas (LNG)) between China and the U.S. In our view, global LNG trade will adapt. We view that the delivery patterns of Russian, Iranian and Venezuelan crude oil has comfortably adjusted. In sum, most commodity producers have seen a version of this movie before and have a playbook for how to react.

Nevertheless, the net impact of recessionary fears have overwhelmed any moderation in the tangible impact of tariffs and uncertainty has spiked.

Indicators to Watch

As suggested above, the key factor to watch for our industries is how demand evolves—particularly in China. Most economists are now predicting a recession that should lead to an erosion of demand. But we would also watch several other macro factors including:

  • How credit markets ultimately react to this.
  • How quickly (or if) negotiations of tariffs settle and with whom.
  • Stimulus measures announced by major economies such as China or the EU.
  • The announcement of supportive positives for the U.S., including deregulation, tax relief, Federal Reserve easing and revamped fiscal spending.

Commodities: Weaker Dollar May Shift Tides After Demand Shock

Roland Morris

Impact

Very simple, commodities fell sharply because demand expectations collapsed. Most commodity indexes were close to all-time highs on April 2. The UBS Constant Maturity Commodity Total Return Index fell 6% in the following two days, a large move for a broad-based index. Copper, the commodity with a “PhD in economics”, declined 14% in two days after making new all-time highs in late March.

These tariffs are much higher and worse than expected. The outlook for global growth has collapsed. Gold has held up better than most commodity sectors but has pulled back about 4% from the new all-time highs reached on April 2. I believe that Trump’s global tariff war is very bullish gold.

Indicators to Watch

The dollar. I think that this trade war is very bearish the dollar in the longer term. If I am correct, commodities should be supported and could resume a bullish trend.

Emerging Markets Bonds: Spreads Pressured, EM Currencies Steady

Eric Fine

Impact

Tariffs and their associated recession risks are having three main effects on emerging market bonds.

  1. The “easy” one: spreads should widen on dollar-denominated bonds. Credit spreads are extremely correlated in risk-off moments like this, and long credit (from investment grade to high yield) has been the most consensus view, for arguably decades. We’d also note that corporate bonds can get incredibly illiquid and that this risk is almost always un-priced, further pressuring spreads higher.
  2. Risk-free yield curves should rally. This means Treasuries, as well as the risk-free yield curves of emerging market local-currency bonds (which were actually up on the year until April 4).
  3. Emerging market currencies have been resilient, only cracking recently when U.S. stocks deepened their declines. EM local currency is incredibly under-owned, which explains their strength this year (despite the selloff in recent days). They also have higher real rates and are often net creditors in dollars, so have FX intervention power. Also CNY has been kept unchanged by the Chinese authorities, anchoring EM currencies.

Indicators to Watch

CNY: watch the daily fix. If it stays largely unchanged (versus the daily model predictions), China is remaining a stabilizing force for emerging markets.

China stimulus: it’s the logical pressure on China, which was already moving in that direction.

The U.S. Federal Reserve: a dovish tilt could mark a turn in trend.

Also, watch the big asset prices combos for the U.S. In particular, if U.S. stocks are down and Treasuries are down, that’s very bad, as that indicates an exit from anything other than cash. If U.S. stocks are down and Treasuries are down and the USD is down, that’s very, very bad, as that is an exit even from cash and the U.S. system.

Emerging Markets Equity: Short-Term Challenges May Accelerate Structural Trends

Ola El-Shawarby, CFA

Impact

The U.S. administration’s proposed reciprocal tariff framework adds a new layer of complexity to the global trade outlook, particularly for export-oriented economies. However, rather than derailing momentum, it may accelerate structural trends already reshaping emerging markets—such as the shift toward domestic demand, innovation-led growth and supply chain diversification.

While tariffs pose short-term challenges for China, they reinforce the country’s strategic pivot toward consumption, high-value manufacturing and technological innovation—including advances in AI and next-generation industries—supported by targeted policy measures. India remains relatively insulated, with growth anchored by robust internal demand and ongoing reform. In Europe, the risk of trade disruption is being met with growing support for coordinated fiscal expansion.

With U.S. exceptionalism increasingly in question, the potential for a weaker dollar and lower rates reduces external pressures on emerging markets. That said, equity market volatility is likely to persist amid uncertain global growth and shifting policy dynamics. In this environment, selectivity is key—we remain focused on resilient, domestically driven businesses with strong fundamentals and clear exposure to long-term structural tailwinds.

Indicators to Watch

Several macro forces are shaping the outlook for emerging markets, and investors should stay focused on a few key areas:

  • Trade and policy dynamics: The broadening scope of U.S. tariffs raises the risk of retaliatory measures and further supply chain disruption, which could weigh on global trade and sentiment.
  • Inflation and interest rates: Trade-driven cost pressures may complicate the inflation outlook. However, a more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve—having paused its tightening cycle—supports the case for rate cuts, improving liquidity and easing pressure on EM currencies.
  • U.S. dollar trajectory: A weaker dollar would provide a meaningful tailwind for EMs by reducing external funding costs and supporting commodity-linked revenues.
  • Structural shifts: Ongoing trends such as nearshoring, supply chain diversification, and rising investment in domestic capacity remain central to EM growth stories.
  • Country-level fundamentals: In a more volatile and uncertain global environment, markets with sound external balances, credible policy frameworks, and scope for targeted stimulus are best positioned to navigate near-term risks and sustain long-term outperformance.

While short-term volatility is likely to persist, these indicators offer a useful lens through which to assess both risks and opportunities across the EM landscape.

Fixed Income: Spreads Widen as Confidence Indicators Flash

Fran Rodilosso

Impact

The newly introduced tariff regime equates to lower growth expectations, at best, and a higher risk of recession both in the U.S. and abroad as a bad, but not even worst, case. Slower growth and/or greater recession odds lead to higher credit spreads, and this impact is being felt across all risky debt markets, including investment grade credit, high yield, loans, CLOs and emerging markets debt.

There were many other market reactions in the couple of days following “liberation day” that are consistent with the rapid move wider in credit spreads, such as the rally in risk-free rates, the fall in commodity prices and, of course, equities entering bear market territory. We find all of these moves consistent with slower growth, lower earnings and—if there is not a rapid reversal in the tariff policies—multiple contraction. What is extremely interesting, and should be of some additional concern, however, is the bond market selloff on Monday, April 7. A correlated selloff with equities may show additional loss of confidence in the U.S. administration’s overall economic plan and its ability to manage through the multiple challenges of elevated prices, high and rapidly growing debt and economic uncertainty that the administration itself has created.

Indicators to Watch

In the credit space, we will be watching a variety of indicators:

  • The new issue market as an indicator of the health of capital markets.
  • Fund flows as a sign of investor sentiment and market technical.
  • Default rates in the high yield bond and leveraged loan markets as an indicator of financial stress, although these will take longer to develop.
  • Lastly, of course, the performance of the U.S. dollar and Treasuries as indicators of market confidence in the U.S. economy and U.S. institutions.

As always, we are looking for other signs of stress that could potentially lead to credit market dislocations, such as funding market disruptions and wholesale unwinding of leveraged positioning. So far, we have seen no such cracks emerge.

Municipal Bonds: As Costs Pressure Projects, Timing and Pricing Matter

Tamara Lowin

Impact

The cost of construction projects across all sectors will increase as tariffs raise the price of materials sourced internationally. Some projects will be shelved, but we expect to see a combination of strained financials as projects are completed over budget and increased revenue streams to close funding gaps—whether higher tolls, tuition costs, rent, healthcare services and/or other fees. Replacing aging infrastructure, from bridges to potholed roads, will be fiscally more difficult to justify. Expansion projects will also likely be stalled without increases in revenue streams, and areas could find themselves with insufficient resources, such as hospital beds and transit.

Public borrowers without expansion or replacement plans will also feel additional strain due to operational supplies and employee costs. Healthcare providers especially have already seen an increase in general supply costs, many sourced from overseas, and in addition, have attempted to address the U.S. shortage of nursing staff with international hiring, which will become more difficult, further squeezing margins and closing more beds.

Sales and use tax revenues are likely to remain within range due to increasing base prices balanced by lower anticipated tourism and domestic demand. Entities directly involved in international trade—such as ports, airports, energy and the state and local governments that depend on the revenue, economic activity and jobs from those facilities—will see strain from higher unemployment (lower income taxes and high unemployment costs) and lower revenue.

Indicators to Watch

Understanding the timing and any guaranteed pricing for existing construction projects as well as realistic pricing projections for future plans will be important to evaluate the feasibility of their success.
