data.title: Bank Of America Q1 Earnings Preview: Guidance And The Macro Don't Align
data.description:
Introduction

Bank of America Corporation (NYSE:BAC) is set to report its Q1 2025 earnings results on April 15 before the market opens. On January 16, the bank issued strong guidance both for FY 2025, and for the first quarter. The management expected NII of $14.5B-$14.6B in Q1 2025, which was higher than the analyst estimation of $14.4B.

Since then, market sentiment has shifted dramatically, raising questions about whether the banks will stand their ground in 2025 if economic turmoil continues. And while the outlook for the full year 2025 doesn’t look that promising anymore, many are curious to know what the possible outcome of the next report is.

BAC stock is now down more than 20% YTD and ~15% over the last month alone, making it one of the worst performers among its peers. For comparison, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC) fell by only 7% and 8%, respectively, over the same period. The numbers themselves aren’t shocking given the market fear, but the fact that BAC is falling harder than peers like JPM and WFC raises some serious questions. And with all the macro pressure building up lately, it’s no surprise that expectations for this report are pretty low.

Company Overview

Bank of America is the second-largest financial institution in the U.S., with different operational segments like consumer banking, global wealth and investment management, global banking, and global markets. In Q4 2024, BAC reported strong top-line growth, with total revenue reaching $25.35 billion, beating estimates by ~$220 million. EPS came in at $0.82, also beating expectations by $0.05.

According to the previous presentation, the bank had solid growth in deposits and lending, with the consumer banking segment showing an especially positive trend.

Although the guidance and the last earnings promise some positivity, in the last 90 days, analyst sentiment regarding BAC shifted to the negative territory, with 11 downward and zero upward revisions to EPS estimates.

The revenue estimate for the next BAC earnings came at $26.91 billion and GAAP EPS of $0.82. While that looks healthy on paper, it’s important to pay attention to the revisions, which show some real caution. Those revisions most likely signal concern about short-term profitability and macro conditions. Meanwhile, revenue expectations have gone in the opposite direction: 8 upward revisions and 0 downward. This shows the confidence in the strength of the Bank of America in interest-related income but also suggests that while results could beat the estimations, profitability will remain under pressure.

About the profitability, BAC guided for Q1 2025 NII of $14.5B to $14.6B, slightly higher than Q4’s $14.5B and above Visible Alpha’s consensus of $14.4B. Management expects sequential NII growth through the year, reaching $15.5B to $15.7B in Q4, which sounds really promising if it can be reached under current conditions.

In the 2025 guidance, the management underlined the fact that Q1 includes two fewer days of interest accrual compared to Q4 2024, cutting about $250M from NII. It basically means that this quarter has two business days less than the previous one, and it is expected to affect Net Interest Income by ~$250 million in a negative way.

It’s important to remember that the inflation problem is not over. There is a high possibility that the U.S. will enter a recession in the upcoming months, and as a result, the Fed keeps pushing rate cuts further down the road. Bank of America’s forecast for how much money it will earn from interest (NII) is basically built on a very optimistic scenario. They made their 2025 guidance based on the information we had back in January. Back then, if you remember, everyone assumed a few rate cuts throughout 2025, no one talked about the recession, and the economy was relatively stable, or at least stabilizing.

Currently, every week that passes makes me question those assumptions and the ability of the company to provide strong results. On the other hand, this huge market downturn began only at the beginning of April, and the first quarter ended on March 31st when the fear wasn’t so enormous.

Regarding the previous point, we have some news from the CEO. In a March 19 interview, Brian Moynihan said U.S. consumers are still spending strongly, with Bank of America customers putting 6% more money into the economy year-over-year.

Our Bank of America consumers are putting 6% more money into the economy than they did for the first two and a half months of last year, and that's a pickup from the fourth quarter of last year at 4-4.5%

However, he also acknowledged a growing sense of pessimism among consumers and small businesses. BAC economists project U.S. GDP growth will slow to ~2% in 2025 and estimate tariffs will shave ~40 basis points off that number. The Fed is expected to hold rates steady for the rest of the year. All of these headwinds could compress BAC’s margins in the second half, but the first quarter supposed to be fine—or at least, the CEO did not mention any misses.

Bank of America economists expect U.S. economic growth to slow to ~2% this year from the "high 2s", and estimate that tariffs will be a ~40-basis-point hit to growth, "especially in the first part of the year." He considers trend growth to be ~2%.

Since rate cuts are expected to be delayed, I suggest we take a look at the noninterest income (like wealth management fees or trading revenue) because it could support the earnings of the company. While noninterest income in Q4 was $11B, which looks like a 37% increase year-over-year, it’s not the full picture. This big jump is mostly because of a one-time charge that happened in Q4 2023. It was the BSBY cessation charge (related to the phasing out of the Bloomberg Short-Term Bank Yield Index, a benchmark rate that was discontinued). So, when you adjust for that one-time charge, the increase in noninterest income is only 15%, not 37% year over year, but down from $11.4B in Q3.

This metric could both win and lose from the investor fear and market uncertainty. Lower market activity could hurt the income of the bank, but at the same time, rising fear could bring in more clients for wealth management because many investors were seeking guidance and wanted to reallocate assets or rebalance their portfolios. So, I suggest we stay open-minded and don’t jump to conclusions too soon.

On the expense side, BAC guided for Q1 noninterest expenses of $17.6B, which includes $0.6B–$0.7B in seasonal payroll-related costs. For full-year 2025, the bank expects noninterest expenses to rise 2%–3% from 2024. If inflation doesn't go down (and if tariffs will stay, the inflation most likely will only grow) or regulatory costs keep climbing, this could harm the earnings more than investors expect. It’s one of the metrics investors tend to overlook, right until it hits the earnings report and tanks the stock.

BAC expected full-year 2025 net charge-offs to come in at 50–60 bps. Pay attention to the smaller letters, where it says: “Assumes no material shift in macroeconomic environment.” It means that this guidance is basically built on the hope that nothing major goes wrong in the economy. No surprise jump in unemployment, no credit shock, no recession. Well, with all the recent developments and rising risks around commercial real estate and consumer credit, that’s a pretty optimistic assumption, and I think there is a strong possibility that the management will want to revise some parts of the guidance, including this one.

Valuation

BAC is trading at a forward P/E of around 11, it’s slightly higher than the sector median, suggesting that the stock is about 6% overvalued compared to the sector. On the other hand, it’s a little below JPMorgan’s P/E of 10.99. But here I must say, that in my opinion the valuation matters less. It’s more about the perception of the company and the trust of investors and analysts, and JPM is winning the game right now.

JPMorgan is considered the gold standard in the banking sector, and while the macro risks are the same for both of these companies, analysts have much more confidence in JPM, and we can see this from the revisions. Remember we started from discussing a bunch of negative revisions for BAC? JPM had 10 positive and zero negative EPS revisions, with 7 up and 1 down revenue revision. It doesn’t look great for the BAC and I will explain why.

Right before the tariff news hit the market and everything flew down, I wrote an article on JPM, claiming that the catalyst for growth might not be the growth but the market panic if we enter the recession. Even though JPM looks overvalued, we see that the stock outperforms the market in the last two weeks, which indeed means that investors trust this bank more than the BAC and are ready to take it as a defensive play against the uncertainties. If we go by the numbers, BAC is expected to post GAAP EPS of $0.82 on $26.91B in revenue, while JPM is expected to report $4.64 EPS on $44.05B in revenue.

Another rather silly influence on BAC stock that I am considering is the fact that JPM will report earnings this Friday, and honestly, I think the market will compare, especially if JPM shows strong results. If JPM comes out strong and BAC just delivers “okay,” it won’t be received well. The stock has already crashed ~15% in the last month, much more than JPM’s ~7% decline, so it’s fair to say a lot of fear is already baked in. But if they don’t reassure investors with guidance or some smart decisions from the management to stay afloat, I don’t see a meaningful rebound just yet.

Even a decent report might not be enough if it’s accompanied by a weak forward outlook, especially if JPM delivers strong, and I think it will. This setup feels fragile. I wouldn’t be adding BAC stock to my portfolio just yet, I would prefer to wait for the freshest information and by this, to cut non-systematic risk, reconsidering after the earnings.

What I’ll Be Watching For in This Report:

  • Will management reaffirm their optimistic full-year NII guidance, or do we get the first signs of a pullback?
  • Any uptick in credit provisions or updated commentary around consumer credit, commercial loans, or CRE?
  • Do they still see consumer spending holding up, or did something change in the last couple of weeks?
  • Do they feel the pressure or the opportunity from noninterest expenses?
  • In general, I would like to hear confidence in the management's tone and the reassurance that even in these uncertain times, they have a plan, and they are ready to bring it into life.

Conclusion

Bank of America walks into this report with a lot to prove. The full-year guidance they gave earlier this year sounded great at the time, but since then, everything in the economy and on the market has started to wobble. The stock has underperformed, inflation doesn’t go anywhere, interest rate cuts are getting pushed back, and fear level gets to pre-COVID times, according to the VIX index.

That doesn’t mean the company is bad. The company is doing fine, but it’s not great either. It could have been priced lower, or it could have had positive revisions before the earnings, but this did not happen. It just means this might not be the best moment to buy in. A decent report probably won’t be enough to turn things around unless the tone is bullish, and they stick to their guidance. If they show any sign of weakness, especially around credit or expenses, I think we’ll see more downsides ahead.

As always, that’s just my take. If you’re a long-term investor, and you believe in the recovery, this might look like a buying opportunity. But personally, I would wait, and this is the reason why I rate BAC stock as a Hold.

data.title: Newmont Vs. Agnico: The Better Buy Amidst Skyrocketing Market Uncertainty
data.description:
The past one year has been truly remarkable for the price of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and other precious metals. Very rarely do we witness the price of gold skyrocketing by nearly 30% in just 12-months, and it appears that the current period would be comparable to the early 1930s, 1970s and early 2000s—all periods of time when significant shifts occurred in the global economy and the monetary regime.

While I always prefer a more direct exposure to gold for long-term investors, gold mining stocks offer a leveraged play on the price of the precious metal and as such, could be superior investments if one gets the timing right.

In addition to market timing, investing in gold mining stocks also requires sifting the winners from the losers.

The past year was a clear illustration why this is so important, even during raging bull markets, both for equities and gold. The reason why I say that is because two of the largest gold mining stocks—Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (NYSE:NYSE:AEM) and Newmont Corporation (NYSE:NYSE:NEM) delivered vastly different returns. The former rewarded shareholders with a total return of more than twice that of gold, while the latter underperformed gold by a wide margin.

Even if we ignore Newmont's quarterly difficulties from late last year, the stock is still underperforming AEM since the beginning of 2025.

That is why it is crucial to always stick with the winners in the gold mining space, as operational or strategy missteps could be costly to investors even when all the stars are aligned for the gold mining sector more broadly.

With that in mind, we should avoid extrapolating past returns into the future, and when comparing AEM and NEW, things are not straightforward.

Focus On Risk

One of the main competitive advantages of AEM has been the company's very low jurisdiction risk with operations concentrated in Canada, Australia, Mexico and Finland. This low risk was one of the main points behind my initial investment thesis on AEM back in 2022. Since then, the stock delivered a total return in excess of 150%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) returned only 30%.

In that regard, Newmont is still inferior to AEM, with a significant proportion of the company's Tier 1 Assets being located in higher risk jurisdictions across Latin America and Africa.

In my view, this dynamic would continue to weigh on Newmont returns over the long term, especially as geopolitical tensions across the globe continue to escalate, and many protectionist policies become mainstream.

A major problem for NEM is that higher gold prices and more geopolitical risks often go hand in hand. This dynamic significantly increases the probability of Newmont having future problems in its higher risk jurisdictions, if the price of gold continues to make new highs.

Cost Structure Makes A Difference

Another apparent advantage that AEM has over NEM is its cost advantage.

The all-in sustaining costs (AISC) of the two companies were nearly identical back in 2019, but the gap has widened significantly in 2023 and remained wide during fiscal year 2024. Over the past year, NEM's AISCs increased by 5%, while those of AEM jumped by only 2%.

Over the past year, investors have been expecting this gap to at least narrow-down as NEM is restructuring and streamlining the business. However, the recently provided 2025 guidance was quite disappointing, with AISC of the Core Asset Portfolio expected to increase significantly (see the graph below). That was the reason why NEM share price plunged following the company's Q4 and full fiscal year earnings release in February.

In terms of actual gold production, NEM's management expects to produce 5.9 million gold ounces in 2025 which is a major drop from the 6.85m produced in 2024. The drop is primarily driven by the recent asset sales, and management expects to achieve more than 10% over the next few years.

Attributable production for 2025 is expected to be approximately 5.9 million gold ounces (...)

Source: NEM Q4 2024 Earnings Release

So, as we look beyond 2025, we expect to add more than 10% of annual gold production by 2028, as we bring forward the high-quality low-cost ounces from the laybacks and projects that I've just described.

Source: NEM Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript (emphasis added).

Agnico Eagle Mines' management, on the other hand, does not expect a notable increase in AISC in FY 2025. This, however, has been mostly expected by investors and is the reason why the stock did not experience a major change in mid-February, when the company reported its full fiscal year results for 2024.

In terms of production, AEM's management does not expect any material change from 2024. As we could see above, the midpoint of the production guidance for 2025 is 3.4m ounces, which is roughly in-line with 2024 production (see below).

Does Valuation Make A Difference?

Everything said so far would help us in drawing any conclusions when we also take a closer look at how the two stocks are priced against each other.

Both on a price-sales and price-to-cash flow basis, AEM trades at significantly higher multiples. The P/S multiple of AEM is more than twice as high as the one of NEM, which is a major difference. On a forward basis, AEM's P/S is 5.2, which is still significantly higher than that of NEM.

When we consider the aforementioned outlook for production growth of the two companies, it is hard to justify such a big difference in valuations. This leaves margins as a key driver, on top of the jurisdiction risk, which as we said earlier will remain to be a tailwind for AEM.

In spite of the large gap in AISC we saw above, the gap between the two companies' gross margins has narrowed-down significantly in recent quarters.

Due to the AISC guidance for 2025, the market is now expecting this gap in margins to once again expand in 2025, but this is likely already priced-in.

On the other hand, we have Newmont's ongoing restructuring, which is likely to have a meaningful impact on the company's fixed costs and would help offset the expected gross margin drop in 2025.

The program behind this cost and productivity work has three key components. Our G&A costs are too high for our go-forward portfolio. We have a laser focus on ensuring that these costs are reduced, as we transitioned from 17 to 11 managed operations over the course of this year.

Source: Newmont Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript.

Thus, once we consider the relatively small differences between AEM and NEM current EBITDA and net income margins, it is becoming hard to justify such the large difference in valuation multiples that we saw above.

Bringing Everything Together

The ongoing bull market in gold has shown why it is crucial to stick with high-quality gold mining stocks and forego the others. With that in mind, however, I would simply extrapolate recent returns for AEM and NEM and assume that the former will remain a superior investment (not at least in the short-term).

The odds of AEM continuing to outperform NEM in 2025 are quite low, given the large valuation gap between the two and the dynamic between margins and production growth. I would go as far as to say that Newmont Corporation is, in fact, in a better spot to deliver superior returns in the short term.

For long-term investors, however, Agnico Eagle Mines Limited remains the better choice. In spite of the likelihood of a near-term pressure on AEM share price, the company remains the lower risk option for investors. The lower cost structure of AEM is also something that is unlikely to change overnight, and with that, Agnico is in a better position to capitalize on what appears to be a new normal in gold prices.

data.title: Elastic: Rating Upgrade On AI/Cloud Momentum And Better Leading Growth Metrics
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Elastic (NYSE:ESTC) earlier this year in January as I was waiting for growth to show signs of acceleration. Recent developments and forward booking metrics have convinced me that growth should start to accelerate. With ESTC valuation falling to a recent low of just 4.2x forward revenue, I believe the risk/reward is attractive, and hence, I am upgrading to a buy rating.

AI and cloud momentum

Over the past few months, I have done even more work on ESTC and have come to the conclusion that growth is very likely to accelerate soon. Before I get to the metrics, I think the main theme is that ESTC is seeing great momentum in its AI and cloud strategy.

In AI, while management did not disclose the revenue contribution, I think there are sufficient signs to infer that demand traction has been great. In the recent earnings call, management explicitly called out examples of customers using ESTC’s AI features. For instance, a global electronics manufacturer cut retrieval latency in half and improved result relevance using ESTC’s vector search stack, and another Fortune 500 firm expanded usage of the ESTC AI use case. For better reference, the total number of cloud customers using ESTC’s AI functions grew ~13% sequentially in Q3 '25 and 8.2% y/y (4th quarter of y/y growth acceleration).

In my view, the important thing, at this point, is not that ESTC is generating more revenue from its AI solutions, but that it shows that more customers are using ESTC AI features. This is especially important for investors like myself that do not have strong technical knowledge to evaluate ESTC products (i.e., know all the codes). Hence, I see more users of ESTC products, especially large customers, as a “stamp of approval” by the industry. Viewed from another angle, ESTC products must have worked very well, such that large firms are willing to embed ESTC’s AI search-related functions into their operational workflows. This reinforces my previous view that growing adoption of GenAI will be a key growth catalyst for ESTC.

In essence, growing AI adoption demands more “search opportunities” (i.e., run time), which significantly expands the demand opportunities for ESTC solutions. ESTC is well positioned to take advantage of this trend as it has a very effective search product (per management, ESTC delivers the most relevant content to best answer a query). The recent results also provided compelling evidence that ESTC is winning demand on this front, wherein Q2 '25 ended with 1,550 customers (vs. 1,370 in Q1 '25) using Elastic Cloud for GenAI use cases. Notably, around 240 of them are worth >$100k in annual contract value [ACV] (vs. 200 in Q1 '25); new customer commitments with GenAI are almost 2x in dollar volume sequentially; and ESTC signed 3 GenAI deals that are worth >$1 million in ACV.

As for Cloud, this has been an overhang for the stock previously, as Cloud revenue growth decelerated from ~32% y/y in Q4 '24 to 29.8% in Q1 '25 and 25.1% in Q2 '25. As I noted previously, this was primarily due to ESTC’s change in go-to-market [GTM] strategy. However, I think this headwind is likely to be in the rearview mirror. Management noted that they’re seeing more consistent workload ramps across the customer base. Specifically, they said, “Customers are scaling up their workloads faster,” and unlike the last few quarters, growth was described as more broad-based. The reason I am willing to put more weight on management words is because cloud revenue y/y growth accelerated for the first time since Q4 '24, growing 25.5% y/y in Q3 '25—a ~40bps sequential acceleration. While it is still early to say that ESTC has completely turned the corner, this is a very encouraging sign that the GTM transition is largely over, and ESTC is ready to accelerate growth.

An additional detail regarding the GTM transition that I found encouraging was the contribution from ESTC's largest channel partner. Although the revenue contribution fell by 100 bps to 12% from the previous level of 13%, on an absolute basis, this channel partner is still generating a sizable amount of revenue. This tells me that despite the GTM transition, ESTC’s relationship with this large partner is still healthy, and importantly, the channel partner is able to accelerate ESTC’s adoption at scale (i.e., this channel partner was able to grow as fast as ESTC itself).

RPO growth further highlights momentum

Another compelling data point is that ESTC backlog figures have improved. Total remaining performance obligations [RPO] y/y growth accelerated to 16% (vs. 12% in Q2 '25 and 13% in Q1 '25), and RPO bookings grew 21.5% y/y (vs. 20.3% in Q2 '25 and -17.4% in Q1 '25). The fact that both metrics saw acceleration from prior quarters suggests that new deals and expansions are ramping up nicely. Total deferred revenue also grew 21% y/y, which marked another quarter of y/y growth acceleration. While the acceleration is modest, it suggests that customers are committing to the platform.

Valuation

Because of the recent market turmoil, ESTC’s valuation has now reached a very attractive level. With better clarity to ESTC growth acceleration potential, I am modeling ESTC to accelerate growth back to 20% over the next few years, with FY25 y/y growth being the trough—using the midpoint of management’s guidance. If ESTC can accelerate growth as expected, we don’t even need multiples to inflect upwards to achieve attractive returns. I simply assumed ESTC would continue trading at 4.2x forward revenue to get to my share price target of $105. But logically, valuation multiples should inflect upwards as growth improves. If you refer to the chart below, the last time ESTC reported growth of ~20% y/y, the stock traded at ~7-8x forward revenue. Suppose this revaluation happens; the upside is even more attractive.

Investment Risk

While ESTC is not directly impacted by the recent sweeping tariffs, its underlying customers are. Suppose these customers decide to downsize their organization or even go out of business; this will impact the rate of consumption (i.e., using ESTC products), which will impact ESTC’s ability to accelerate growth. Additionally, although I have a view that the GTM transition is largely over, if any hiccups happen in the next few quarters, it may impact the stock sentiment, and the market may think that growth will decelerate again.

Conclusion

My upgrade to a buy rating on ESTC is because I saw evidence that growth is set to accelerate. ESTC is seeing positive momentum in AI and cloud, and forward-looking metrics like RPO and deferred revenue are inflecting higher. With valuation at just 4.2x forward revenue, I believe the setup offers attractive upside.

data.title: OFA Group: No Growth, No Cash Flow, No Thanks
data.description:
Another Hong Kong Company Goes Public

Architects usually draw blueprints, but OFA Group (OFAL) just drew up an IPO instead.

I know, I know—we're not talking about the next AECOM (ACM) or Jacobs (J) here. This Hong Kong player's way more low-key. They offer full architectural services, including interior design and execution for commercial and residential buildings.

Just like BUUU Group (BUUU)—the company behind events like the S2O Songkran Music Festival in Hong Kong—OFA runs on a project-based model, working with construction firms or private clients.

That means revenue can fluctuate quite a bit, so it's something worth keeping in mind.

Certainly, the biggest driver for OFA is Hong Kong. The Jewel of the East saw its number of visitors plummet from 55.91 million to 3.57 million in a matter of a year.

Then came the pandemic, bringing strict restrictions along with it. The next two years were rough for the city, with visitor numbers dropping to just 90,000 in 2021 and only slightly recovering to 600,000 in 2022. A gradual recovery began in 2023, with the city welcoming 34 million visitors year-to-date—a big win, even if it was still just half of the 2018 peak.

The positive momentum seems to have carried into 2024, and all signs point to visitor numbers returning to pre-pandemic levels by 2025.

But is this really that big of a market? And more importantly, is it really growing as fast as OFA would like it to?

According to the company's own IPO document, the Interior Design market is worth $45.1 billion by 2022 and is expected to grow at a CAGR of 7.4%. Doesn't sound disruptive to me. The Architectural Services market is at least 8x larger, reaching $359.9 billion and is expected to grow at a CAGR of 9.8%.

Single-digit growth, huh. Seems small when compared to other IPOs out there. Just yesterday, I was taking a look at Pinnacle (PFAI). I hadn't really been paying much attention to the hydroponics market, but it turns out it's expected to grow at double-digit rates year over year.

The problem for me is that this is a very saturated market in Hong Kong.

It's a huge city, but there's not much room left to grow—unless you're talking about cross-border projects. But here's the thing: China's economy is slowing down. After years of aggressive stimulus, especially in real estate, they've ended up with a bunch of ghost towns.

Also, dealing with the competition from huge firms from Europe and the Americas like AECOM and B+H Architects is not an easy thing to deal with. These companies tend to dominate when it comes to integration with the Greater Bay Area.

It's no surprise, then, that the OFA Group IPO has a bigger goal in mind: international expansion.

There are two regions that OFA Group intends to reach in the coming years:

  1. Asia-Pacific Area; and
  2. United States.

There's a big world outside of Hong Kong!

It's worth pointing out that they already have an office in Rolling Hills, CA.

Still, it's quite small compared to the scale of their ambition here. They want to create a subsidiary in the United States that will serve as a center for expanding services and direct relationships with local customers and partners.

In Asia, they're eyeing places like Singapore and Malaysia—regions where population growth is picking up speed.

If you think the fundamentals aren't great, wait until you see the financials—they're downright alarming.

Keeping An Eye On The Numbers

Like I mentioned earlier, OFA Group leans a lot on its contracts, since the whole business is built around a project-based model. And you can clearly see the slowdown from FY 2023 to FY 2024. It's never a great sign when you're looking at an IPO and the company's already posting declining sales.

Now, if they want to convince you to buy into the idea while it's still in its early stages, the least they could do is show some momentum—or at the very least, not be heading in the wrong direction.

Just a reminder, FY for OFA ends at the end of March, so we will have to look at the trends on an annual basis first and then analyze the next six months ending in September 2024.

During FY 2024, OFA saw sales of $530,606. This represented a drop of at least 51.72% year-over-year from where they were at $1.09 million in FY 2023.

Breaking down the revenue, it's clear that the lion's share—80.51%—comes from Design and Fit-out. Project Management makes up just 11.03%, and Application even less at 8.46%.

So, at this point, they're essentially a design company more than anything else.

It turns out that OFA Group generated $174,942 in gross profit. Since operating expenses far exceeded this, they reported an EBIT of -$87,339. In FY 2023 they reported an EBIT of $161,985.

After interest payments on debt (yes, OFA Group has debt on its balance sheet), they reported a net loss of -$93,197. In FY 2023 net income was $174,268.

In both years, OFA Group didn't post a single positive operating cash flow. In other words, they're not generating cash—mainly because they poured a fair amount of capital into operating assets and saw a drop in operating liabilities, which only added more pressure.

That is, it went from -$184,692 in FY 2023 to -$239,111 in FY 2024. Since they don't invest anything in Capex, they end up taking on debt to cover the cash shortfall. In FY 2024, they issued $472,477 in long-term debt. This ended up increasing their cash by $233,366 to $268,160.

But make no mistake. If they didn't issue debt, this cash would be burned through in less than two years.

The problem here isn't even the receivables—unlike what we saw with BUUU. In fact, OFA needs liquidity because it can't show a positive OCF! If they hadn't issued long-term debt, they'd be burning through cash so fast it wouldn't last long—just over two years if they kept spending like they did in FY 2023, and less than a year at the FY 2024 pace.

Note that this is a profitability problem, not a payment cycle problem. Sure, reducing receivables would reduce the deficit a bit, but it wouldn't offset the net loss.

So, this little over $6.75 million would serve two purposes:

  1. Maintain a liquidity cushion while reporting net losses and hoping for a turnaround—without having to issue long-term debt, since they can't even cover the interest through operations; and
  2. Invest in international expansion. Of course, this will cost money, and if they don't generate OCF, how would they spend more on Capex? This would generate an even bigger cash deficit!

Looking at the numbers, we have a number of reasons not to invest in this IPO regardless of the offering price:

  1. Limited and competitive market;
  2. Sales sinking more than 50% year-on-year;
  3. Low profitability;
  4. Negative OCF given the net loss and to a lesser extent due to the management of operating assets;
  5. No self-financing investment; and
  6. Long-term debt sitting on the balance sheet isn't necessarily a bad thing—unless you can't afford to pay the interest, which is exactly the case here.

But has that changed in the six months that followed (March to September/24)?

It seems like nothing has changed. In fact, it seems like things have gotten worse. Sales in the six months were just $125,591, down 53.45% from $269,842 in the same half a year ago. Net loss was -$192,862, significantly lower than the same half a year ago -$61,496.

So it's safe to say that things haven't gotten any better.

The IPO Offer: Is It Worth Getting Into?

Basically, they're aiming to raise $6.7 million by issuing 1,500,000 shares at $4.50 each. After the IPO, there'll be 11,111,111 common shares outstanding (or 11,336,111 if the underwriters fully exercise their option to purchase additional shares).

I'm sticking with the first scenario.

Fundamentally, the red flags are hard to ignore. But then comes the real question—does the price make it worth a shot?

Since EPS is negative, we'll use high-growth multiples here.

Starting our analysis with P/S, let's define the size of the decline for FY 2025 in sales. Remember, I'm following this path not just by the decline in FY 2024 sales, but by the decline in the first half of FY 2025. Let's model a year-over-year decline based on that. Since the first half dropped 53%, we'll assume a full-year decline in the range of 45% to 55%.

This would give us sales ranging from $291,383 to $238,222. Since there are 11.11 million shares outstanding post-IPO, we would have an RPS of $0.02.

Considering the $4.50 offering, the P/S ratio would be 225x! This IPO is definitely one to walk across the street and never look back on!

But let's consider a scenario here: they report similar FY 2023 sales of $1.09 million. That would give us an RPS of $0.09. The P/S ratio would be 50x.

That's still super expensive. Consider that AECOM, which secured the Northern Metropolis contract, trades at a P/S of 0.7x, while the industry median is 1.22x.

Another metric we can use is EV/S. With an EV of $50.37 million, we would have an EV/S in a range of 172x to 211x. Likewise, when we go back to FY 2023 sales, we would have an EV/S ratio of at least 46x. The industry median for EV/S is 1.62x!

I think you get the red flags here. If we want to see a P/S of 2x (which I consider high, but acceptable on a case-by-case basis), sales would have to be at least $25 million, almost 25 times the reported revenue in FY 2023.

That said, this is an IPO to avoid at all costs. In my opinion, it is extremely expensive and will most likely trade for much less in the secondary market. I have a 'Strong Sell' rating.

Risks To My Bear Thesis

As much as I'm pricing in a bearish scenario based on the company's current trend, we still need to consider what could actually make this IPO take off.

Hong Kong is expected to return to pre-pandemic visitor levels by 2025. Sure, the real tourism boom was back in the 1990s, but if China's economy starts picking up steam again, mainland Chinese tourists—who make up the bulk of Hong Kong's visitors—could have more disposable income and a stronger appetite for travel.

In such a scenario, we could see visitor levels trend upwards above pre-pandemic levels. This could lead to a resurgence of interior renovations and new construction in the city, benefiting the OFA.

There's also a chance that going international brings upside I'm not currently modeling. We all know the US is a tough nut to crack, but the picture looks a bit friendlier in ASEAN. Places like Singapore and Malaysia could offer real growth potential for OFA.

Either way, I think we're making the right call by sitting this IPO out. Just to put it in perspective, OFA would need to grow its FY 2023 revenue by 25 times just to justify the current P/S at the offer price. That's a stretch!

data.title: Meta: Market Too Busy Selling To Notice Its Tariff Insensitivity Or Llama 4
data.description:
META stock: Llama 4 and tariff impacts

My analysis on Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) stock was published a bit more than 1 month ago. That analysis, under a title of "Meta Platforms: Price Pullback Made It A GARP Pick,” recommended META stock as a buy. As stated in the title, the key consideration at that time was its growth-adjusted valuation. More specifically, I argued that:

META’s capex layout updated in the Q4 earnings report (ER), when contextualized by its depreciation and amortization, suggests that its true economic earnings are far better than its accounting EPS. These considerations, when combined with the recent price correction, have made META an excellent GARP (growth at a reasonable price) stock. The ER also reported a large cash position of $77.8 billion, providing opportunities for acquisitions and making its valuation metrics even more appealing.

Since then, it is not an exaggeration to say that the overall investment landscape has undergone a tectonic shift caused by the tariff conflicts. META is not immune to the turbulence. As such, I think it is a crucial time to reexamine META stock due to the unique nature of its business. I agree with the prevailing view that higher tariff rates are a headwind in general for equity. But I think the ongoing market broad selloff has ignored the granularity of penitential impacts on specific stocks and also business-specific catalysts.

Against this backdrop, the remainder of this article will concentrate on the top two new developments in my mind since my last writing. The first one is, of course, the potential impacts of higher tariffs. On this front, I will argue that there are key factors that can make META stock less sensitive to higher tariffs compared to the overall economy. The second one is a business specific catalyst, the release of its new Llama 4 AI model.

I will argue that the current valuation has totally ignored the potential of these catalysts. As such, I see a significantly wilder price-value gap in META stock now compared to my last writing, resulting in a rating upgrade to strong buy.

META stock: earnings exposure

Let me first start with the elephant in the room: the potential impacts from higher tariffs. I see multiple factors that could make META stock to be less sensitive to higher tariffs compared to the overall economy. First, META’s profits are predominantly digital revenue.

The chart next provides a breakdown of its revenue sources. As seen, the vast majority of Meta's revenue, almost 98% of it, is attributed to advertising within its "Family of Apps." A much smaller portion of the revenue comes from "Reality Labs", accounting for 1.30%. Higher tariffs primarily affect the cost of physical goods, from what I’ve learned. Thus, unlike the many other companies that rely on the physical import or export of goods, Meta's core business is less directly impacted by tariffs on tangible products.

Second, Meta also boasts a vast global user base across its platforms (such as Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, etc.). To wit, the following donut chart illustrates its revenue share by region for the year 2024. Admittedly, the largest contribution to Meta's revenue comes from the United States & Canada, accounting for 38.42%. However, note that is a case of relative majority, with the North America contributing less than (actually far less than) 50%. The Asia Pacific region is the second-largest revenue source, generating 27.36% of the total. Europe contributes 23.32% to Meta's revenue, while the "Rest Of the World" accounts for about 10.9%.

I expect this highly geographically diversified model to buffer the impact of tariffs imposed by or on specific countries or regions. I anticipate tariffs to affect economic activity in different regions/countries to different degrees. Thus, the impact on Meta's overall revenue in one region (say the U.S.) might be partially offset by continued growth in other regions.

META stock: Llama 4 in focus

Now let’s move to a key business-specific catalyst. On April 5, META released its new Llama 4 AI models. As you can see from the following chart, Llama 4 includes a suite of AI models emphasizing speed and efficiency. More specifically, the Llama 4 family includes Llama 4 Behemoth, Llama 4 Maverick, and Llama 4 Scout. Behemoth, the largest and “most intelligent model” according to META, is still in the “Preview” stage. Both the Maverick and Scout are available already. META called these models “the beginning of a new era of natively multimodal AI innovation.” Putting aside the marketing hyperbole, I do see a few good reasons behind this bold statement.

For example, the next chart presents instruction-tuned benchmarks for Llama 4 Maverick compared to a few key competing models (including Google’s (GOOGL) Gemini 2.0 Flash, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) V3.1, and also ChatGPT’s GPT-4o). AI model comparison is always a multidimensional topic, and I am sure there are various ways to interpret the data here. My own overall assessment is that Llama 4 Maverick provides a quite competitive package of performance and cost. As seen, in terms of inference cost, Llama 4 Maverick ranges from $0.19 to $0.49, while competing models cost in the range of $0.17 to $4.38.

For key multimodal tasks (such as Image Reasoning and MathVista), Llama 4 Maverick also achieved better scores than competing models. It is also worth noting that Llama 4 Scout is quite lightweight yet features very compelling capabilities. The model features 17 billion active parameters and 109 billion total parameters, but can fit onto a single Nvidia H100 GPU. As such, I won’t be surprised to see wide adopting of this model in many applications where high-end systems are not available or cost-effective.

Other risks and final thoughts

As a hotly debated stock on the Seeking Alpha platform, I won’t delve into the risks that are more generic to META (such as competition risks, regulation risks, etc.), Here, I will just point one more positive and also a downside risks are more particular to the thesis presented in this article. The additional positive is its attractive valuation. The recent selloff has contracted its P/E significantly. To wit, the chart below shows its P/E ratio in the past 5 years, from 2020 to the present. As of this writing, META’s P/E ratio stands at 21.34, noticeably below its historical average of 25.29.

My view is that this discount is a reflection of the market’s overaction, which ignored the differentiating factors in its business model and the potential impacts of tariff rate changes. On the negative side, one of the factors I argued above is that META’s reliance on digital revenues helps to dampen its sensitivity to tariff rates. However, its heavy reliance on digital revenue (especially digital ad revenues) creates its own risks too. In the case of an economic downturn (either caused by higher tariffs or other developments), the advertising budgets of various business could shrink. Thus, META could still feel the tariff impact in an indirect way.

All told, my verdict is that the new developments since my last writing have materially shifted the return/risk ratio for META. This article detailed the top two developments in my mind: one macroscopic (the impacts of higher tariffs) and one business-specific (the release of its new AI models). Combined with the P/E contraction, these developments have made the META stock a much more compelling investment than at the time of my last writing, prompting me to upgrade its rating to a strong buy.

data.title: Inland Empire PMI: This Kind Of Dynamic Sprinkles Additional Cold Water On The Idea Of A Looming Recession
data.description:
Inland Empire PMI spikes to highest since early 2021, New Orders spike to record: Private sector is dynamic, but not always in linear ways.

Corporate frontrunning of tariffs is now a big thing. And it’s creating activity in the US goods sector where companies are trying to get goods into the US before tariffs are applied, and where companies in the US are trying to shift more production to their plants in the US, from their plants in Mexico or elsewhere, and to suppliers in the US. Which is a strategy that, for example, automakers are now trying to implement where they can, though it takes time to do that. Providing an incentive to move more manufacturing to the US is what tariffs are all about.

Consumers may also try to front-run tariffs with big-ticket items, which, along with heftier tax refunds this year, may explain why new vehicle sales rose to the highest level for any Q1 since 2019, on hot growth in March, after solid growth in January and February.

These efforts would be a boost for the US economy as long as they persist. Goods are only a relatively small part of the economy, with services being the vast majority (about two-thirds of GDP), and services are not directly impacted by tariffs. But manufacturing matters a lot, for all kinds of reasons, and there are now shifts underway that may not always be simple or linear or predictable.

There’s more evidence of that. The Inland Empire PMI tracks companies in the goods-producing and goods-handling sectors in the “Inland Empire” in Southern California, an industrial region that ties Asia via the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach to the rest of the US. As with other PMIs, a value above 50 means expansion, the higher the value above 50, the faster the expansion. A value below 50 means contraction.

The overall Inland Empire Manufacturing PMI spiked to 65.7 for March, the highest since the reopening burst of the economy in the first half of 2021, reflecting “robust expansion in the region’s manufacturing sector,” according to the Institute of Applied Research at the California State University, San Bernardino, which produces the PMI.

Quite a reversal from the weakness seen in December (42.5). For comparison, the dotted purple line denotes the national ISM Manufacturing PMI by the Institute for Supply Management. In January, the ISM PMI had seen the first significant expansion since 2022, and in February, it still showed an expansion though at a slower pace. But in March, it fell into a mild contraction (image via the Institute of Applied Research).

The Production Index rose to 67.9, the third month in a row of growth, “signaling a sustained recovery in production activity across the region.” It was also higher than in March 2024 and March 2023.

The New Orders Index spiked to a record 78.6, the third month in a row of sharp acceleration, “signaling robust demand,” the report said.

“However, it remains to be seen whether this level of activity can be sustained,” the report notes, perhaps based on the theory that this spike in orders could reflect corporate frontrunning of the tariffs, and frontrunning tends to entail a hangover later. The red dots mark the Marches (image via the Institute of Applied Research):

The Employment Index jumped to 67.9 in March, from 59.4 in February, the third month in a row of accelerating expansion. “The continued upward trend indicates that the recovery in the manufacturing job market is gaining strength,” the report noted. And it added, perhaps with an eye on this frontrunning situation: “While the sharp increase reflects a notable pickup in hiring activity, it remains important to monitor whether this pace of growth will continue in the coming months.”

The Commodity Price Index declined to 67.9, from 75.0 in February, meaning that commodity prices are rising but at a slower pace than in the prior month. And overall input costs remain “elevated.”

The Supplier Deliveries Index rose to 57.1 in March, from 50.0 in February, indicating slower deliveries and worsening “supply chain delays” compared to the previous month.

“Economic challenges and emerging opportunities”

Despite the growth in business, there were a lot of concerns among the executives about the tariffs and trade policy uncertainty - “top of mind for many respondents,” the report noted - along with worries that the surge in business activity might not last.

“With ongoing tariff uncertainty - particularly affecting the import of raw materials and the export of goods - sectors like logistics, manufacturing, and agriculture are expected to be disproportionately impacted. As a result, the region is likely to face a mix of economic challenges and emerging opportunities, creating a more dynamic business environment,” the report noted.

“While potential policy changes may sound alarming, it may also create new opportunities for specific industries or sectors in the region, capitalizing on them in a sustainable way will require coordinated efforts and strategic long-term planning,” the report said.

The private sector in the US is very dynamic, adjusting to changing situations in myriad ways to make the best of it and take advantage of opportunities. And this kind of dynamic sprinkles some additional cold water on the idea of a looming recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alamo Group: Nearing An Upgrade
data.description:
From my experience, investors are turned off by companies that are experiencing a period of weakness. Value investors, however, don't necessarily have a problem with declining revenue and profits. It all depends on the outlook for the company and how cheap you can get shares for.

One company that I was getting closer to upgrading last year from a "hold" to a "buy" was Alamo Group Inc. (NYSE:ALG). For those not familiar with the business, it serves as a producer and seller of vegetation management equipment and industrial equipment. Because of how things have been as of late in the agricultural industry, revenue for the business as a whole has been declining. However, shares were getting cheaper, almost to the point of justifying an upgrade.

At the end of the day, I decided not to pull the trigger on that move. Since then, the company has actually outperformed my expectations to some extent. While the S&P 500 (SP500) is down 16.5%, shares of Alamo Group are down 16.8%. We continue to see weakness, but I believe that the market is getting close to overreacting to this. I'm not yet ready to pull the trigger, especially in light of recent uncertainty caused by geopolitical issues. But in all honesty, depending on how those turn out and depending on the agricultural sector moving forward, an upgrade might just be around the corner.

Tough Times Continue

The most recent data that we have regarding Alamo Group covers the fourth quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. This represents one additional quarter compared to when I last wrote about the business in December of last year. During the most recent quarter, revenue for the company totaled $385.3 million. That's 7.7% below the $417.5 million reported the previous year. This was driven entirely by the firm's Vegetation Management segment, which saw revenue plummet 25.5% from $214.4 million to $159.8 million.

This is the part of the enterprise that produces a wide array of agricultural equipment. Examples here include tractor-powered equipment like rotary motors, finishing mowers, disc mowers, backhoes, rotary tillers, post hole diggers, and more. It also sells snow blowers, rock removal equipment, grass cutters, remote control mowers, self-propelled sprayers, agricultural seed-bed preparation cultivators, industrial grass mowers, cutting blades, stump grinders, tree care and forestry equipment and attachments, and a lot more.

To be honest with you, this is not the first time that the Vegetation Management segment has experienced weakness. From 2023 to 2024 as a whole, revenue for the unit dropped from $979 million to $785.2 million. That's a decline of 19.8%. According to management, this was mostly because of the forestry, tree care, and agricultural mowing markets. This is not particularly surprising to me.

As an example, one firm that we could look to for some interesting data is PotlatchDeltic (PCH). As the image below illustrates, pricing for the timber that it produces has been on the decline. Weaker demand in the construction space caused by high interest rates and broader economic uncertainty can be thanked for this. Furthermore, during the COVID-19 pandemic, and immediately after, lumber supply increased quite a bit and that created an oversupply of inventories.

The farming industry has faced significant pressure in recent years also. After seeing a spike in profits after the end of the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a significant decline in profitability. As I pointed out in a previous article about a different firm, and as you can see in the image below, net farm income dropped to $142.44 billion last year. That's the lowest we have seen since 2020. It is forecast to decline to $180.11 billion this year. However, I do believe that there is significant risk here when it comes to tariffs, the impact those could have on the broader economy, and immigration.

According to the American Farm Bureau Federation, the U.S. exported $176 billion of agricultural products to 189 different countries and territories last year. 47% of those exports found their way to Mexico, Canada, and China. In fact, around 20% of all agricultural products produced in the U.S. are exported. Tariffs and restrictive immigration policies will certainly increase costs on farmers while simultaneously reducing the demand for American exports. It stands to reason that this could pose meaningful short-term risks for Alamo Group and companies like it. Reduced farm income here at home and weaker demand for our exports should in theory cause some pain.

By comparison, the Industrial Equipment segment of the company has performed much better. In the final quarter of last year, revenue was $225.5 million. That was up from the $203.2 million reported the previous year. And for the year as a whole, revenue of $843.3 million beat out the $710.6 million reported for 2023. This rise in sales was thanks to strong demand for excavator and vacuum trucks, sweepers and safety products, and snow removal equipment.

However, the prospect of government budget cuts and a slowdown in the industrial sector does pose risks for the business. We have already seen backlog decline year over year, dropping for this segment from $507.7 million to $481.5 million. That's not as bad as the decline from $352.1 million to $187.1 million for the Vegetation Management segment. But any sort of decline is a net negative.

Because of the decline in revenue, profits for the business also contracted. Net income pulled back slightly from $31.5 million to $28.1 million. On an adjusted basis, it fell a similar amount from $31.5 million to $28.9 million. Other profitability metrics were mixed. One really significant bright spot for the company was operating cash flow. It shot up from $54.2 million to $79.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we also get an increase from $41.7 million to $45.2 million. However, EBITDA for the business did fall from $58.9 million to $50.2 million.

It is worth noting that something that has helped the company has been cost-cutting. The Vegetation Management segment has been a key focus of the business, with the goal of cutting annual run rate costs by between $25 million and $30 million. Some of those savings came in the second half of 2024. It seems as though a big chunk of these cost savings involves consolidating its forestry plants, its U.S. agricultural plants, and its decision to cut its global staff by roughly 14%.

For the year as a whole, revenue contracted from $1.69 billion to $1.63 billion. This brought with it an unfortunate decline in profits from $136.2 million to $115.9 million. As the chart above illustrates, adjusted net profits fell from $136.2 million to $121.9 million. The company also saw a drop in EBITDA from $247.7 million to $220.6 million. While the firm did enjoy a rise in operating cash flow from $131.2 million to $209.8 million, the adjusted figure for this dipped slightly from $181.6 million to $178.3 million.

If we take the historical results that the company generated in 2023 and 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are multiples that I consider to be nearing the low end of the fair value range. Furthermore, relative to similar enterprises, shares also look attractively priced. In the table below, I compared Alamo Group to five other similar firms. On a price to earnings basis, it was the cheapest of the group. Using the EV to EBITDA approach, we can see that only one of the companies was cheaper than it is, while that number rises to three of the five when using the price to operating cash flow approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Alamo Group 16.0 14.1 11.3
Lindsay Corporation (LNN) 20.3 14.6 12.9
Titan International (TWI) 18.8 4.2 9.3
AGCO Corporation (AGCO) 41.2 10.1 167.7
The Toro Company (TTC) 18.7 12.4 12.2
Deere & Company (DE) 20.6 14.2 14.0

Takeaway

Near term, I definitely believe that there are risks that investors should be cognizant of. However, Alamo Group Inc. shares are getting cheaper, almost to the point of justifying an upgrade. If we can see these broader economic concerns addressed by the powers that be and if shares can fall just a bit further, or fundamental performance improve just a bit more, an upgrade could eventually be justified. But for now, I will maintain a "hold" rating on the stock.
